definition: Claude is powered by a large language model (LLM): a neural network trained on vast text to predict likely continuations of input, which—after alignment training—produces coherent, instruction-following responses rather than raw autocomplete.
layman: Under the hood, Claude reads your message as a string of word-pieces, runs them through a huge pattern-matching brain trained on lots of text, and writes the reply one chunk at a time—like very advanced autocomplete that learned grammar, facts, and conversation.
content:
## LLM foundations (how Claude works at a high level)

You do not need a PhD to use Claude productively—but a **mental model** of what an LLM is prevents common mistakes (treating it like a database, calculator, or oracle).

### Prediction, not lookup

At inference time, Claude's model **does not search a table of pre-written answers**. It **generates** text token by token, each step conditioned on everything before it (your prompt + its own draft so far). Training taught it statistical patterns of language and knowledge embedded in those patterns.

```
Prompt tokens → [Transformer network] → next-token probabilities → sample/decode → repeat
```

### Tokens and context

Text is split into **tokens**—subword pieces, not always whole words. `"Anthropic"` might be one token; `"unbelievable"` might split. Everything counts toward the **context window**: your instructions, pasted documents, tool results, and Claude's reply.

| Concept | Why it matters |
| --- | --- |
| **Token** | Billing, limits, and truncation are token-based on the API |
| **Context window** | Maximum tokens the model can "see" at once |
| **Truncation** | Material beyond the window is invisible—chunk or summarize |

Stage 7 covers token counting and caching in depth; here, remember: **finite attention span**.

### Transformers (the architecture family)

Modern Claude models belong to the **transformer** family: layers that use **attention** to relate each token to others in context. Attention is why Claude can connect a constraint in paragraph one to an answer in paragraph five—when it fits in the window.

You will not configure transformer layers as a chat user; the takeaway is **contextual pattern matching at scale**, not symbolic rule execution.

### Training vs inference (preview)

| Phase | What happens |
| --- | --- |
| **Pretraining** | Learn language and world knowledge from large text corpora (next-token prediction) |
| **Alignment** | Steer behavior toward helpful, harmless, honest responses (see Training overview & Constitutional AI) |
| **Inference** | Your prompt runs forward through fixed weights; the model generates output |

Weights are **frozen** during your chat unless Anthropic ships a new model version—you are not "teaching" Claude permanently from one conversation (product **memory** features, where enabled, are a separate controlled layer).

### What this explains about Claude behavior

- **Fluent but fallible** — patterns can misfire into plausible-sounding errors (**hallucination**).
- **Sensitive to phrasing** — small prompt changes can shift answers (not a bug; a property of generative models).
- **Knowledge is statistical** — cutoffs and gaps exist; see Knowledge cutoff sibling.
- **Not a symbolic math engine** — can reason well on many problems yet still err on arithmetic without tools or verification.

### What to learn next in this branch

- **Training overview** — pretraining and alignment stages without implementation detail.
- **Constitutional AI intro** — Anthropic's principle-based alignment thread.
- **Strengths & limitations** — practical failure modes for users.
keyPoints: Claude generates text token by token from learned patterns, not by retrieving fixed answers.
Tokens and the context window bound how much text Claude can use at once.
Transformers use attention to relate tokens across your prompt and reply.
Pretraining plus alignment produces instruction-following behavior at inference time.
LLM mechanics explain fluency, sensitivity to prompts, and hallucination risk.
